tasks.name: First hive inspection
tasks.why: Confirm the colony survived winter and the queen is laying.

tasks.name: Check honey stores
tasks.why: Colonies can starve in early spring before flowers bloom.

tasks.name: Clear the hive entrance
tasks.why: Remove dead bees and debris so foragers can fly freely.

tasks.name: Check for swarm cells
tasks.why: Spring is prime swarm season — catch queen cells early.

tasks.name: Add a super if colony is growing
tasks.why: Give bees space before they decide to swarm.

tasks.name: Weekly swarm checks
tasks.why: Swarming peaks in early summer — inspect every 7 days.

tasks.name: Add honey supers as needed
tasks.why: Bees fill supers fast in a good nectar flow. Give them room.

tasks.name: Monitor for varroa mites
tasks.why: Mite populations grow fastest in summer. Count now to act in time.

tasks.name: Harvest honey (when supers are fully capped)
tasks.why: Capped honey has low enough moisture — uncapped can ferment.

tasks.name: Ensure a water source nearby
tasks.why: Bees need water to cool the hive in hot weather.

tasks.name: Treat for varroa mites
tasks.why: Autumn treatment protects the winter bees that must survive until spring.

tasks.name: Feed sugar syrup
tasks.why: Colonies need 15–20 kg of stores to survive winter. Feed if short.

tasks.name: Assess colony strength
tasks.why: Weak colonies rarely survive winter. Consider merging with a stronger hive.

tasks.name: Reduce the entrance
tasks.why: Smaller entrance deters robbing bees and mice as the colony shrinks.

tasks.name: Remove queen excluder
tasks.why: Queen must be able to move with the cluster in winter.

tasks.name: Heft the hive weekly
tasks.why: Lift the back edge to feel weight — light means low stores. Feed fondant if needed.

tasks.name: Keep the entrance clear
tasks.why: Dead bees block airflow and cleansing flights. Clear gently without opening.

tasks.name: Avoid opening the hive
tasks.why: Cold breaks the cluster. Only open above 12°C on a calm, sunny day.

tasks.name: Apply oxalic acid treatment
tasks.why: With no capped brood, oxalic acid knocks back varroa mites very effectively.
